name: mol-eval
problem: ML engineers and computational chemists at biotech/pharma companies building AI-powered drug discovery pipelines have no systematic way to validate that their LLM-generated molecular structures are correct. They manually spot-check outputs with ad-hoc RDKit scripts, spending 5-10 hours per week on validation that still misses subtle stereochemistry and graph-topology errors—leading to costly failures when invalid molecules enter virtual screening or wet-lab synthesis queues.
solution: mol-eval is a Python SDK and CLI that provides deterministic, CI-integrated validation for LLM-generated molecular structures. It implements the MolLangBench paper's canonical SMILES normalization with graph-isomorphism cross-validation across multiple molecular representations (linear strings, molecular graphs, stereochemistry) to catch structural errors that naive string comparison misses. Unlike the academic MolLangBench benchmark (a static dataset for model ranking), mol-eval is a production testing framework: define molecular validation suites in YAML, run them against any LLM provider, get pass/fail results in CI, and auto-correct failures via retry-with-corrective-feedback loops.
source_papers: MolLangBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Language-Prompted Molecular Structure Recognition, Editing, and Generation (arXiv:2505.15054)
market_analysis: Target buyers are ML engineers and computational chemists at Series A-D biotech and pharma startups (20-500 employees) building AI-driven molecular design pipelines. The computational chemistry software market exceeds $4.5B growing at 12% annually, with AI-first drug discovery creating an entirely new tooling category around LLM output validation. Distribution is via pip install and Docker Hub; land with individual researchers at $49/mo, expand to team licenses as validation suites become shared infrastructure. Conservative TAM: ~5,000 pharma/biotech companies with active AI pipelines × $5K average annual spend = $25M addressable market for a focused molecular validation tool.
buyer_persona: ML engineers and computational chemists at Series A-D biotech/pharma startups (20-500 employees) building AI-powered drug discovery pipelines—teams at companies like Recursion, Insilico Medicine, Relay Therapeutics, or Isomorphic Labs that use LLMs for molecular generation and editing and need to validate outputs before feeding them into expensive virtual screening runs or wet-lab synthesis.
price_point: $49/mo individual researcher, $39/seat/mo team (5+ seats), $499/mo enterprise (unlimited seats, SSO, priority support, custom validation rule authoring, SLA with 99.9% uptime for hosted API)
competing_tools: Schrödinger Suite ($15K-$100K/yr enterprise molecular modeling, no LLM output validation)
ChemCrow (open-source LLM chemistry agent, no systematic validation or CI integration)
Custom RDKit scripts (5-10 hrs/week × $80/hr = $20K-$40K/yr in senior engineer time)
MolLangBench (open-source academic benchmark, static dataset only, not a production tool)
core_algorithm: Canonical SMILES normalization with molecular graph-isomorphism cross-validation: each LLM-generated molecule is canonicalized via RDKit, then verified against expected output using both canonical string equality and molecular graph isomorphism checking (handling equivalent but differently-ordered SMILES), with multi-representation validation across linear SMILES strings, 2D graph topology, and stereochemistry-aware atom-by-atom comparison—directly implementing and productizing the MolLangBench evaluation methodology that decomposes molecule-language tasks into recognition, editing, and generation with deterministic cheminformatics verification.
api_design: CLI: `moleval check <smiles> [--expect <smiles>] [--strict] [--format text|json]` validates a single molecule against expected output with canonical + graph-isomorphism checks. `moleval test <suite.yaml> [--provider openai|anthropic|ollama] [--model gpt-4o] [--parallel 4]` runs a YAML-defined validation suite against an LLM. `moleval report <results.json> [--format text|json|junit]` produces CI-friendly test reports. `moleval init [--tasks recognition|editing|generation]` scaffolds a starter validation suite. `moleval serve --port 8080` runs HTTP API with POST /v1/validate {smiles, expected, task_type} returning {valid, canonical_smiles, graph_match, errors} and POST /v1/test {suite, provider, model} returning {passed, failed, results[]}.
differentiation: Unlike MolLangBench (a static academic benchmark for ranking models on a fixed dataset), mol-eval is a production developer tool with CI/CD integration, custom YAML test suites, JUnit output, and LLM retry loops for self-correction. Unlike ChemCrow (a general-purpose LLM agent that chains chemistry tools without validation guarantees), mol-eval focuses exclusively on deterministic pass/fail validation—it doesn't try to be an agent, it validates what agents produce. Unlike Schrödinger ($15K+ enterprise suite for molecular dynamics simulation), mol-eval is a $49/mo developer-first tool purpose-built for the LLM-to-molecule validation gap that emerged with generative AI in chemistry.
moat: The multi-representation validation pipeline (canonical SMILES + graph isomorphism + stereochemistry-aware checking) catches classes of errors that simple string comparison misses entirely—achieving the deterministic accuracy the MolLangBench paper demonstrated is necessary. As teams accumulate custom validation suites encoding domain-specific molecular correctness rules, switching costs compound and the library of reusable test patterns becomes a growing competitive asset.
v1_scope: V1 ships the core validation engine (canonical SMILES canonicalization + graph isomorphism matching + stereochemistry checks) and the CLI with `check`, `test`, `init`, and `report` commands—enough to validate LLM molecular outputs deterministically and integrate into CI pipelines via YAML test suites with JUnit output. OUT of scope for v1: the HTTP server (`serve` command), LLM-integrated editing/generation with retry loops, molecular image parsing, 3D conformer validation, provider-specific prompt optimization, and enterprise features (SSO, audit log, hosted API). Thirty-second demo: `pip install mol-eval && moleval check 'C(=O)O' --expect 'OC=O'` prints 'PASS: canonical match (both canonicalize to C(=O)O)' proving deterministic multi-representation validation works out of the box.
